steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure around the blank
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses: “Nakalembe doesn't just compile the information” (complete thought with subject and verb) and “she also shares her findings with African farmers” (another complete thought). These clauses need to be separated by appropriate punctuation. The word “though” is being used to introduce a concessive or contrasting relationship—acknowledging the first statement while presenting an additional point.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule for independent clauses
steps.content: A semicolon separates two independent clauses. Since both parts of this sentence are structurally independent—each with a subject and verb—they require a semicolon between them. The “though” here functions as a subordinating element that softens the relationship between the two ideas, showing that the second clause is being presented despite or in addition to the first. The correct punctuation is a semicolon directly before “though,” with no comma after it when the following clause is already clear in structure. That matches choice C — C is correct.
